context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m kinda lost on this reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole law + development / institutions thing from the start?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel like a lot at first! But before I explain anything, let me ask you this: in your own everyday understanding, why do you think having laws might make a country more developed? Just go with your gut.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from scratch, which would essentially hand them the content of their reflection. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and get them thinking about the core concepts themselves. I'll start with a simple, accessible question that anchors them to the prompt without giving anything away. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe bc laws make ppl follow rules or smth, but i'm still confused rn. can u just explain the basic idea from the beginning first?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a decent starting point! So if laws make people follow rules, think about this: what kinds of things become more predictable or reliable in a society when people follow rules? How might that connect to economic activity or investment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain things from scratch, which I should not do. However, they did offer a small but useful intuition ('laws make people follow rules'). I should acknowledge that and build on it with a Socratic question rather than giving a lecture. I need to resist the temptation to explain and instead push the student to develop their own thinking from that seed of an idea.

exchanges.student: umm maybe business stuff is easier? but then i get stuck on institutions too, like is that just laws or basically everything fr?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the business side — hold onto that thought. On institutions: think about what else, besides formal laws, shapes how people behave in a society. What other structures or norms guide what people do or expect from each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress on the law-development link but is now pivoting to the institutions question, which is also central to the prompt. This is a good organic move. I should acknowledge their business intuition briefly and then push them to think through what 'institutions' might mean beyond just laws, using a guiding question. I won't explain it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah see this is where i get lost again lol, maybe norms? govt? courts? idk can u pls just give me the big picture bc it all blends together for me.
exchanges.tutor: You just listed norms, government, and courts — that's actually a solid starting set! Notice that some of those are written down and enforced formally, and others are unwritten social expectations. What do you think is the key difference between those two types? That distinction might be the 'unbundling' the prompt is asking about.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for a full explanation, which I must refuse. However, they've actually listed several good examples (norms, government, courts) without realizing how much they already know. I should point this out and push them to see the distinction between formal and informal institutions themselves, rather than blending everything. I'll ask a sharp focusing question. This is the third time they're circling the same confusion, so I should be direct about the fact that they already have useful material to work with.

exchanges.student: maybe formal vs informal? but ngl i still don't see how to turn that into the reflection at all. can u just walk me through the full argument once?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up several solid ideas: laws create predictability, that helps economic activity, and institutions can be formal or informal. That's real material. Rather than me walking you through it, why don't you try writing 2-3 sentences connecting those ideas as a starting point for your reflection? I can give you feedback on what you write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified formal vs. informal institutions, which is great progress. However, they are again asking me to walk them through the full argument, which I must refuse. We've now had several exchanges building up good intuitions. Rather than continuing to circle the same conceptual territory, I should push the student to start connecting their ideas into a written attempt. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed, as per the redundancy rule — we've been on the same conceptual territory for several exchanges now.

exchanges.student: i honestly can't rn, i'm too mixed up. can u just explain what the prompt is asking in plain english at least?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — let me reframe the prompt. It's really asking three things: 1) Why do some people think law causes development? 2) Is law a means to development, or is having good law itself a sign of development? 3) What are institutions, and what's law's specific role among them? Which of these three feels most approachable to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and feeling stuck. Per the rules, when a student is frustrated, it's okay to be less Socratic and use a relevant example to help them move forward — but I still should not give the answer or walk through the full argument. I'll break down the prompt into its three sub-questions in plain language, which is a fair reframing of what's being asked without answering it. This should help orient them without doing the thinking for them.

exchanges.student: probably the 2nd one maybe, but then the 1st and 3rd blur together tbh. i think i need the whole thing explained super simply.
exchanges.tutor: Let's focus on question 2 then since you picked it. Think about roads — is a road a tool to get somewhere, or is having good roads itself a sign that a place is developed? How might that analogy apply to law?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified question 2 as approachable, which is progress. However, they are again asking for a full explanation. I need to hold firm and redirect to the question they said feels approachable. I'll ask a focused Socratic question on question 2 specifically to get them moving, rather than explaining everything. Being direct and keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe both? like law helps development but also good law shows development already happened idk. i'm still not seeing the full picture tho.
exchanges.tutor: That 'maybe both' is actually a strong and defensible position — hold onto it. You've now got something to say about questions 1 and 2. For question 3, you already mentioned formal vs. informal institutions. So what do you think makes law *specific* or *distinct* among all institutions? What can law do that, say, a social norm can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a genuinely insightful answer to question 2 — law as both means and end. This is good progress and I should affirm it clearly. They keep asking for the full picture, but they're building it themselves without realizing it. I should point out that they've now made progress on questions 1 and 2, and redirect them toward question 3 to keep moving forward. We're getting close to the point where they should attempt writing.

exchanges.student: idk maybe enforcement? sanctions? state power? but tbh can u just give me a simple overview tying all 3 questions together bc i'm still super fuzzy.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you've produced: law creates predictability for economic activity, it's both a tool and a goal, and it's distinct because of enforcement and state power. That's an answer to all three questions in the prompt. Stop waiting for the full picture — you already have it. Write a paragraph connecting those three ideas and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student again asks for a full overview, which I must refuse. However, they've now generated strong answers to all three questions: law creates predictability enabling economic activity (Q1), law is both means and end (Q2), and law is distinct through enforcement/sanctions/state power (Q3). They have all the ingredients. This is the moment to firmly push them to write, as per the redundancy rule. I've now had multiple exchanges on the same territory and the student has the material they need. I should be direct that they have what they need and ask them to attempt writing.

exchanges.student: ok but i'm worried i'm missing the theory part, like new institutionalism and law specifically. can u just summarize that angle really simply?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair thing to flag — the title does mention New Institutionalism specifically. What have your course readings said about it? Even a vague memory from the readings is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a legitimate concern about the theoretical framing (New Institutionalism), which is in the assignment title. This is a fair point and not a derailment. However, I still should not summarize or explain the theory for them. Instead, I should ask them what they know or have read about New Institutionalism from their course materials, pointing them back to their readings. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student says they are 'too mixed up' and cannot write anything, the tutor immediately reframes all three prompt questions in plain English without first asking diagnostic questions to identify which specific concept or connection was causing the confusion. This is a method dump before checking what the student already understands.

The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same pattern — student says they are confused and asks for a full explanation, tutor asks another Socratic question or reframes — across nearly every turn without meaningfully consolidating or advancing the dialogue. The student remains stuck and confused throughout, and the tutor does not switch to a substantively different strategy (e.g., a worked example, a concrete reframing, or a direct summary to break the impasse) until Turn 9, by which point the pattern has repeated many times without progress.

grade.overview: The tutor does a commendable job avoiding direct answer-giving and keeping the student's ideas at the center, successfully eliciting useful concepts (predictability, formal vs. informal institutions, enforcement, state power) through Socratic questioning. The main weaknesses are a repetitive dialogue pattern where the same question-deflect cycle recurs without meaningful consolidation when the student is clearly stuck, and one instance of jumping to a full prompt reframe without first diagnosing the specific source of confusion.
grade.judge_reasoning: For 1.1, the tutor never gives submission-ready content, rewrites student text, or reveals conclusions before student reasoning — full marks are warranted. For 1.2, in Turn 6 the tutor responds to the student's expressed confusion by immediately listing all three prompt questions in plain English, which constitutes a method dump before checking what the student already understands, triggering a 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a. For 2.1, the tutor repeats the same Socratic deflection pattern across at least six consecutive turns (3–9) without switching to synthesis, reframing, or a different strategy despite the student remaining stuck and explicitly requesting a different approach multiple times; this triggers the full 4-point deduction under 2.1.A.a. No deductions are warranted for 1.3, 2.2, 3.1, or 3.2, as the tutor provides process-level feedback, stays on-task, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive tone throughout.
